coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical immunosensor for individual and simultaneous determination of Cytokeratin fragment antigen 21-1 and Neuron-specific enolase using carbon dots-decorated multiwalled carbon nanotube electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Filik, Hayati
Avan, Asiye Aslıhan
Altaş Puntar, Nilay
Özyürek, Mustafa
Çakıcı, Maşide
Güngör, Zeynep Banu
Kucur, Mine
Kamış, Handan
coredata.dc:description: In this approach, we fabricated a sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor for individual and simultaneous sensing of cytokeratin fragment antigen 21-1 (CYFRA 21-1) and neuron-specific enolase (NSE) using the multiple-label concept. In this report, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from citric acid and ethylenediamine (EDA). A hybrid EDA-CDs-MWCNTs composites were prepared and then decorated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The obtained hybrid composites were used as an immunosensing platform. Furthermore, AuNPs decorated EDA-CDs-MWCNTs was carefully loaded with reactive dye (orange G and carminic acid) and utilized as signal labels. The surface morphology and the electrochemical properties of the modified SPCE were studied carefully. The linear range for the individual and simultaneous detection was 0.002–10 ng mL−1 for the CYFRA 21-1 and NSE and the detection limits (LODs) were 0.22 pg mL−1 and 0.15 pg mL−1 for the individual detection while 0.25 pg mL−1 and 0.20 pg mL−1 for simultaneous detection, respectively. This developed immunoassay was utilized for the assessment of two markers in the serum sample with reasonable consequences.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs.
Schematic representation of the preparation process of the immunoprobes (A) and CYFRA 21-1 and NSE immunosensor (B).
TEM images of EDA-CDs (A), ox-MWCNTs (B), and EDA-CDs-MWCNTs (C).

(A) The EIS spectra of SPCE (a), AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (b), anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1),anti-NSE(Ab1)/AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (c), BSA/anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1)/anti-NSE (Ab1)/AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (d), Antigen (CYFRA 21-1 and NSE)/BSA/anti-CYFRA 21–1(Ab1)/anti-NSE (Ab1)/AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (e), AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@(OG/CA)-anti CYFRA 21-1 (Ab2)/anti NSE (Ab2)/BSA/Antigen (CYFRA 21-1 and NSE)/BSA/anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1), anti-NSE(Ab1)/AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (f) in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- (1:1) solution containing 0.1 M KCl (frequency scan from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz with 5 mV amplitude). The insert in (I) shows the EIS spectra in the higher frequency range. The insert in (II) shows an equivalent circuit model applied to fit the Nyquist plots. Rct, charge transfer resistance; Cdl, double layer capacitance of the electrode; ZW, Warburg impedance resulting from the diffusion of ions; RS, electrolyte solution resistance. Nyquist plot of experimental (colored symbols) and fitted results (lines). (B) Cyclic voltammograms of 3 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] with 0.1 M KCl electrolyte at bare SPCE (a), AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (b), anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1), anti-NSE (Ab1)/AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (c), BSA/anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1), anti-NSE (Ab1) -AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (d), Antigen (CYFRA 21-1/NSE)/BSA/anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1), anti-NSE (Ab1)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (e), and AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@(OG/CA)-anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab2), anti-NSE (Ab2)/BSA/Antigen (CYFRA 21-1 and NSE)/BSA/ anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1), anti-NSE (Ab1)/AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (f) at scan rate 50 mV s−1 from potential −0.4 V to 0.6 V.

SWV for various concentrations of (A) CYFRA 21-1 and (B) NSE, in the range 0.002–10.0 ng mL−1 in 0.1 M HAc/NaAc (pH 5) at anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1), anti-NSE (Ab1)- AuNPs- EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE. Simultaneous analysis performance of the sandwich-type immunosensor (C). Determination of solutions containing different concentrations of two antigens. From (0.002) to (10): 0.002, 0.01, 0.05, 0.08, 0.1, 0.5, 2.0, 5.0, 10 ng mL−1. Calibration curves have been plotted for each antigen individually (A and B) and simultaneously (C), as shown in Fig. 10 (A, B, and C insets). The error bars represent the standard deviations of three parallel tests. SWV parameters: pulse size 150 mV, frequency 5 Hz, and step size 4 mV.
Determination (control test) of CYFRA21-1 and NSE in standard serum samples.
Detection of CYFRA21-1 and NSE in real human serum samples with the developed sandwich-type immunosensor and ELISA methods.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical immunosensor for individual and simultaneous determination of Cytokeratin fragment antigen 21-1 and Neuron-specific enolase using carbon dots-decorated multiwalled carbon nanotube electrode

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs.

In this approach, we fabricated a sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor for individual and simultaneous sensing of cytokeratin fragment antigen 21-1 (CYFRA 21-1) and neuron-specific enolase (NSE) using the multiple-label concept. In this report, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from citric acid and ethylenediamine (EDA). A hybrid EDA-CDs-MWCNTs composites were prepared and then decorated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The obtained hybrid composites were used as an immunosensing platform. Furthermore, AuNPs decorated EDA-CDs-MWCNTs was carefully loaded with reactive dye (orange G and carminic acid) and utilized as signal labels. The surface morphology and the electrochemical properties of the modified SPCE were studied carefully. The linear range for the individual and simultaneous detection was 0.002–10 ng mL−1 for the CYFRA 21-1 and NSE and the detection limits (LODs) were 0.22 pg mL−1 and 0.15 pg mL−1 for the individual detection while 0.25 pg mL−1 and 0.20 pg mL−1 for simultaneous detection, respectively. This developed immunoassay was utilized for the assessment of two markers in the serum sample with reasonable consequences.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the world, cancer is one of the most prominent health issues. Early detection of cancer helps a greater chance of remedy, survival, and quality of life for the patient and can minimize the cancer death proportion. Cancer biomarkers (CB) are crucial biological molecules (or bioparticles). They can comprise most biomolecules utilized for medical objectives. CBs are cultured by human tissues or tumor cells and their merits can designate the phases of cancer progress [1–4]. Cytokeratins are proteins made by specialized cells named epithelial cells. Cytokeratin 19 (CK19) fragment 21-1 is a biomolecule of lung cancer helpful for estimating clinical identification. It is a water-soluble moiety of CK19 that is released in cells and serves as a cancer marker in some malignancies [5,6]. The CYFRA 21-1 level are 0–3.3 ng mL−1 in serum [7,8]. On the other hand, the grade of CYFRA 21-1 has a more vigorous relationship with propagations and survival possibilities than NSE in all cancer patients. Moreover, pre-treatment CYFRA 21-1 values are seriously correlated with development status and phase of malady. On the other hand, abnormal CYFRA 21-1 level is a negative indication signal for patients with non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) [7,8]. NSE has been called a particular cancer biomarker in SCLC treatment and the value of NSE has become an important indication for premature identification and follow-up therapy. In healthy people, the NSE values are between 5 ng mL−1 and 12 ng mL−1 in serum and this value is 20 ng mL−1 in the cerebrospinal fluid. Besides, when the serum NSE value exceeds 35 ng mL−1, SCLC is suspected. [9–11]. Accordingly, the combined sensing of various cancer markers is urgently required to improve the diagnosis of lung cancer.

To date, various conventional immunoassay techniques for CYFRA 21-1 and NSE have been declared such as chemiluminescence immunoassay (CLIA), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), radioimmunoassay (RIA), and fluorescence immunoassay (FIA) method [1–4,11]. Electrochemical immunosensors (ECIs) are powerful tools widely utilized in clinical applications. Nanomaterials have been widely utilized for the fabrication of ECIs. A powerful tool in increasing of efficiency of the immunosensor is the addition of a permanent chemical modifier or the functionalization of the nanomaterial base of the ECIs. To date, a large number of various nanocomposites have been applied in the design of ECIs to reach high sensitivity. Besides, they are characterized by a fast response, cost-effectiveness, and simple operation. Nanoarchitectures have shown up as a fascinating class of materials that are in high demand for a range of practical implementations and various nanotextures could also expedite charge transfer and improve the conductivity and catalytic ability of invented ECIs, thereby advancing the current signal of the target analyte. Therefore, nanomaterials-based ECI has opened a new door for cancer diagnosis and therapy. Up to now, various ECIs have been developed for the individual sensing and quantification of CYFRA 21-1. Main modification nanomaterials used included graphene (3D–Gr)/chitosan (CS)/glutaraldehyde (GA) composite [12], 3D graphene/gold nanoparticles [13], Si3N4/MoS2/MWCNTs [14], zirconia decorated reduced graphene oxide [15], poly(thionine)-Au composite [16], l-cysteine capped lanthanum hydroxide nanostructures [17] and Ag nano-ink [18]. Besides, Wang and Ma [19] synthesized a redox hydrogel by a cross-linking coordination approach utilizing phytic acid as a chelating agent and Pb(NO2)2 as the gelatinizer. The prepared electrode platform was modified by electrochemical deposition of AuNPs onto the surface of the hydrogel and in this protocol, CYFRA 21-1 was picked as a target analyte to be monitored. For individual electrochemical sensing of NSE, several nanotextures have been exploited in sensor invention and coating to further increase the analytical merit of the C by combining specific physical and chemical attributes from Au/CuxO@CeO2 nanocubes [20], PtCu [21], NiWO4 [22], graphene oxide/polyaniline film [23], Zirconium-porphyrin complex [24], p-phenylenediamine/graphene composite [25], ionic liquid/imprinted polymer [26] and so on.

Only single biomarker quantification has low estimation levels due to the lack of specificity between a single cancer marker and lung cancer. To accurately diagnose lung cancer in premature phases, multimarker are generally identified. Quantification of multimarker is of a great deal of importance due to its high sensitivity, small assay time, and low cost. CYFRA 21-1 and NSE are two crucial markers of lung cancer and are usually concurrently evaluated in the early diagnosis. Therefore, simultaneous detection of biomarkers has attracted intensive attention, which can impressively improve the assay performance and lower the sample expenditure. So far, a few ECIs have been invented for the simultaneous analysis of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE. In this regard, Wang et al. [27] synthesized an Au-poly(o-aminophenol) (Au-PoAP) and Au-poly(p-phenylenediamine) (Au-PpPD) by a one-pot strategy and utilized it to design a sandwich-type ECI for simultaneous sensing of three biomarkers (CEA, CYFRA 21-1, and NSE) of lung cancer. Furthermore, Wang and Ma [28] developed chip-like GCEs composed of three GCEs fixated in a parallel for the label-free sensing of three serum tumor markers (namely CEA, CYFRA 21-1, and NSE). Cheng et al. [29] fabricated a multianalyte FET (field effect transistor) immunosensor that allows the label-free sensing of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE, for lung cancer diagnosis. The FET immunosensor reached the sensing of CYFRA 21-1 in serum sample at the low level of 1 ng mL−1 but was faced with a critical limit to define 20 ng mL−1 NSE in serum. The FET sensor has a linear dynamic range from 1 ng mL−1 to 1 μg mL−1 for both analytes. Shan and Ma [30] employed different polymer-gold nanoparticles as signal tags to develop ECIs for simultaneous sensing of five cancer markers (CEA, NSE, CA125, CYFRA 21-1, and SCCA). Recently, Stefan-van Staden et al. [31] have developed stochastic sensors based on diamond paste matrices modified with different electroactive modifiers like dextrins and porphyrins, and ones based on three metals like Au, Cu and Ni. All the stochastic sensors have been employed for the simultaneous sensing of NSE, CEA, HER-1 and CYFRA 21-1 from whole blood samples.

During simultaneous immunoassay, the most crucial difficulty is to get electrochemical redox-active species, which could create distinctive redox peak currents devoid of interference in a sole cycle. To date, the most utilized redox-active species are conventional reactive dyes such as Prussian blue, thionine, ferrocene, toluidine blue, methylene blue, and so on. Apart from these, divalent heavy metal ions such as Cu2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, and so on have also been employed [3,32–35]. These redox-active species (dye and metal ions) can be mostly used for two analytes detection. However, the peak potentials of redox-active organic compounds (especially reactive dyes) are close, which makes them insufficient to be concomitantly utilized in multianalyte (three or four analytes) immunosensing. Nevertheless, it is still now a major challenge to realize multiple biomarker quantification owing to a lack of redox-active species. Besides, the used redox-active species principally require to be anchored on nanostructures such as carbon, metal, and magnetic nanostructures for further antibody coupling and current response amplification, which are involved in a complex process. For this reason, the finding of new redox-active materials by a new examination is of great matter for two or more biomarkers' quantification in a single run.

Among various nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great interest in electroanalytical applications. In the last years, many CD preparation models have been manifested. Based on the carbon sources (chemical or green precursors), the CD preparation models are generally split into two types: “top-down” and “bottom-up” [36,37]. CDs possess unusual physical and chemical attributes due to their high surface area and nanoscale size. Specifically, the small size of CDs may provide a large surface area for more convenient electrocatalytic activity. Typical, CDs are constituted of an sp2-/sp3-carbon core and an external layer carrying plentiful functional groups such as − NH2, −OH, and − COOH. These oxygen-rich groups and defects in CD strongly influence its electronic, electrochemical, and mechanical behaviors [38]. Since CDs have a hydrophilic outer surface, this makes it immensely inconvenient for electrode modification or fabrication. Therefore, CDs have been employed as a response amplifier with other nanomaterials, conductive polymers, or compounds. Until now, there are only two papers existing on the preparation of an ECI based on CDs for early cancer diagnosis. For example, Huang et al., [39] fabricated an aptasensor for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) sensing based on Pb2+ dependent DNAzyme-supported response magnification on CDs-Nafion-ionic liquid (CDs-Nafion-IL). In 2021, Sri et al., [40] fabricated an electrochemical immuosensor for TNF-α sensing based on CD-poly methyl methacrylate (PMMA)/indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode.

Matrix is a crucial component of an immunosensor application. Function-wise, a matrix includes an immunosensing layer of captured species (antibody-antigen). Therefore, it is one of the major components of the immunosensor fabrication footstep. In this study, ethylenediamine passivated-CDs decorated MWCNTs (namely EDA-CDs-MWCNTs) was prepared as an efficient alternative immunosensing platform for the early tumor marker assessment. The as-synthesized EDA-CDs-MWCNTs were further decorated with AuNPs (AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs) and the prepared hybrid composites were utilized as an immunosensing platform. Moreover, the as-prepared AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs were separately loaded with orange G and carminic acid to achieve two distinguishable redox probes. The prepared probes were also used as redox-active labels to form a sandwich-concept immunosensor for the individual (single) and simultaneous electrochemical assessment of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE.

Human NSE antigen and mouse monoclonal anti-NSE antibody acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Monoclonal anti-Cytokeratin 21-1 antibody and CYFRA 21-1 antigen was acquired from Abcam (UK). All reagents (antibodies and antigens) were preserved in a deep freeze at −20 °C. The raw-MWCNTs were used without extra purification and bought from Metrohm-DropSens chemical company. HAuCl4·4H2O, 1,3-Naphthalenedisulfonic acid, 7-hydroxy-8-(phenyl azo)-, disodium salt (orange G, OG), 1,3,4,6-tetrahydroxy-9,10-anthraquinone (carminic acid, CA), trisodium citrate, ethylenediamine (EDA), NaOH, KCl, NaCl, NaH2PO4·2H2O, KMnO4, citric acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and Tween 20 were used in the experiments and the mentioned reagents were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The K4[Fe(CN)6].3H2O/K3[Fe(CN)6] were from Merck (Germany). The phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was prepared from Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, and NaCl (each 0.1 M). HAc/NaAc (Acetate buffer) was prepared from CH3-COOH and CH3-COONa (each 0.1 M). PBS (0.1 M) containing 0.05 % Tween 20 (PBST, pH 7.0) was used as a wash buffer. BSA blocking solution was prepared as a 1 % (w/v) solution in PBS and control human serum were purchased from Merck (Normal human serum collected off the clot from healthy normal humans). All of the chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and the test solutions were carefully prepared with distilled water. Human serum samples (biosamples) for scientific purposes were collected from the Istanbul University-Cerrahpaşa Hospital (IUCH) (Ethics Committee (ID: 09.04.2019/56111) (Istanbul/Turkey).

A Gamry Reference 600 potentiostat/galvanostat/ZRA was used for voltammetric measurements. The modified SPCE (Metrohm DropSens) was used for the quantification of biomarkers in serum samples. SPCE (4 mm diameter, 50 μL volume) and the counter electrode is made of carbon and the reference electrode is made of Ag/AgCl. The prepared composites were characterized by SEM (ZEISS EVO 10, Carl Zeiss Microscopy GmbH, Germany) and FTIR (ATR-FTIR Spectrum Two, Perkin Elmer, USA). The internal texture of the composites was examined by a high-resolution FEI TALOS F200S TEM, operated at 200 kV. The ox-MWCNTs and EDA-CDs-MWCNTs are characterized by Raman spectroscopy using a Renishaw in Via Raman Microscope with a 100 mW 532 nm He–Ne laser with excitation line set to λ = 514.57 nm and explore time is 5 s. 100 mW was focused on the sample with a 5x objective.

The oxidized MWCNTs were carefully prepared from raw MWCNTs by a modified approach. The oxidation of MWCNTs using KMnO4 was performed [41–43]. The KMnO4 oxidized MWCNTs exhibit a huge portion of carboxyl (–COOH) groups compared to the other ox-MWCNTs, also a relatively small quantity of sidewall destruction [42,44]. Therefore, in this work, MWCNTs were properly oxidized by an H2SO4/KMnO4 mixture. For the oxidative reaction of MWCNTs, initially, H2SO4 (1.0 M, 20 mL) was added to raw-MWCNTs (100 mg) in a reaction flask. After ultrasonic distribution for 30 min, the MWCNTs suspension was subsequently heated under reflux for 4 h. Then, solid KMnO4 (10 mg) was carefully incorporated into the suspension under vigorous stirring. In this case, KMnO4 was utilized as an oxidant in the presence of H2SO4. The obtained mixture was further heated under reflux for 4 h, cooled, washed by decantation (until it reached pH 7), filtered, and then, washed twice with methanol and dried at 50 °C overnight.

Carbon dots were prepared using the previously declared procedure [45] with some modifications. EDA-CDs derived from ethylenediamine and citric acid. The EDA molecules have been approved to serve double roles as N-doping precursors and surface passivation agents [45,46]. In this regard, the EDA-CDs were prepared from EDA-citric acid using a facile one-step microwave pyrolysis. For the synthesis of the EDA-CDs, 1 g citric acid was diluted with 10 mL distilled water and then mixed with EDA (390 mg) under vigorous stirring. Then, the clear solution was put into a domestic microwave oven (700 W) and irritated for 2 min and cooled to room temperature. The carbonization proceeded very fast and no acid or inorganic salt was added. The obtained red-brown and bubbling solid was dissolved with distilled water (10 mL) and dialyzed against pure distilled water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 100) for 3 days. A homogeneous suspension of EDA-CDs was preserved in the dark at normal temperature for further experiments.

The as-synthesized EDA-CDs were decorated on the sidewalls of ox-MWCNTs through reflux [46,47]. Briefly, about 2.0 mL of EDA-CDs was mixed with 10 mL of the ox-MWCNTs solution (10 mL, 0.1 mg mL−1) and the prepared mixture was properly refluxed at 90 °C for 2 h and cooled down to ambient temperature and the obtained products were centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm and washed twice with distilled water and the EDA-CDs-MWCNT composites were dispersed well in 10 mL (1 mg mL−1) distilled water.

Fig. 1 displayed the preparation process of AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs. We synthesized AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNT composites according to the literature with slight modification [48,49]. In this context, the AuNPs were anchored on the EDA-CDs-MWCNTs surfaces by reduction reaction. Typically, the negatively-charged AuNPs were prepared by reducing HAuCl4 with sodium citrate in the presence of EDA-CDs-MWCNTs. In brief, the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs architectures were made ready as follows: 10.0 mL of EDA-CDs-MWCNTs dispersion (1.0 mg mL−1) was heated under reflux conditions for 30 min. During this process, the reflux temperature is near the boiling point of the water. Then, HAuCl4 (0.1 %, 5 mL) was added and gently refluxed for an additional 10 min. Next, sodium citrate (1 % w/v, 5 mL) was speedily added to the glass reactor. The reduction of AuCl4 ¯ to AuNPs was finished under reflux for 30 min and the colloidal mixture was cooled to normal temperature and separated by applying centrifugal force (5000 rpm for 5 min). The products (AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs) were appropriately washed with distilled water several times to remove the non-attached AuNPs and the products (namely AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs) were rehydrated in distilled water (10 mL). During this process, the negatively charged AuNPs were anchored on the surface of EDA-CDs-MWCNTs owing to the attraction between the positively charged amine groups (–NH2 groups in EDA-CDs-MWCNTs) and the citrated AuNPs (negatively charged) together with the van der Waals interaction.

Fig. 2 A displayed the preparation process of immunosensing probes. In this context, AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG composites were prepared as follows: 1 mL of AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs (1.0 mg mL−1) was mixed with OG (1.0 mL of 2 × 10-3 M) and diluted to 5 mL with 0.1 M PBS (pH 5) and then stirred vigorously for at least 5 h. During this process, OG reactive dye was bound to the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs composite via π-π stacking [50,51]. Then, the obtained AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG hybrids were separated by using centrifugation. Afterwards, the collected products were washed with distilled water to remove the non-integrated OG dye. AuNPs loaded EDA-CDs-MWCNT composites have a high concentration of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups, which enable more efficient adsorbing of OG onto the surface. Also, we synthesized AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@CA hybrids according to the procedure described above. The collected composites were carefully re-dispersed in 2.0 mL of distilled water and used as a redox probe.

Furthermore, 100 µL (1 mg mL−1) anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab2) was gently added to the above composites (AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG) and stirred at 25 ± 1 °C for 2 h. During this process, the detection antibody (anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab2) could be anchored to the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG. After centrifuging and washing several times, the anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab2)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG bioconjugates were reacted with 400 µL of 1 % BSA (w/v) solution at 37 °C for 2 h. Next, the above dispersion was centrifuged and carefully washed with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7). On the other hand, the anti-NSE (Ab2)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@CA bioconjugates were also prepared using a similar procedure. After that, the reached bioconjugates were re-dispersed in 1.0 mL PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.0) and stored in the refrigerator and thus kept below 4 °C.

An ECI is a crucial biosensing platform for the accurate quantification of target biomarkers. The proposed method for the fabrication of the immunosensor was based on drop-casting the synthesized hybrid on the surface of an SPCE. The production workflow of the immunosensor can be illustrated in Fig. 2B. 10.0 µL of AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs (1.0 mg mL−1) was dropped on the SPCE surface and dried at natural temperature. Then, the immunosensor platform was incubated with a mixture of 100 ng mL−1 anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1) and 100 ng mL−1 anti-NSE (Ab1) solution at 4 °C overnight. During this process, AuNPs can capture various active biomolecules such as antibodies. As a result of this process, the anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1) and anti-NSE (Ab1) could be anchored on the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs-SPCE surface. After being washed with PBST (0.01 M, pH 7.0) to remove excess antibodies (Ab1), the immunosensor platform was washed again with PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.0). Subsequently, the prepared ECI was gently incubated in a solution of 1 % BSA (w/v) at 37 °C for 1 h. BSA is usually the utilized blocking component to prevent nanoparticle aggregation and unbeknown binding to other molecules. Afterwards, to remove the excess BSA, the immunosensor platform was washed with 0.01 M PBST and 0.01 M PBS (pH 7.0), respectively. Eventually, a sandwich-type ECI for simultaneous detection of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE was successfully achieved and the immunosensor platform was denoted as anti-CYFRA21-1 (Ab1), anti-NSE (Ab1)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE and stored at 4 °C. In this study, three different immunosensor platforms were utilized for CYFRA21-1 and NSE detection at each concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement procedure of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE antigen
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2B showed the fabrication process of the electrochemical immunosensor of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE determination. To achieve the antibody-antigen reaction and electrochemical measurement, the immunosensor platform (namely anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1)/anti-NSE(Ab1)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs /SPCE) was first incubated with the mixture of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE solutions for 50 min at 37 °C and then washed with buffer solutions. After that, the mixture of 1:1 diluted anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab2)- AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG and anti-NSE(Ab2)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@CA redox probes (a 5-µL aliquot of solution) was cast onto the immunosensor platform (modified SPCE) surface carefully and dried in air to evaporate the solvent for 1 h. Consequently, a sandwich-type immunoassay was formed (Ab1-antigen-Ab2). After each step, the immunoelectrode surfaces were carefully washed with 0.01 M PBST (pH 7) and 0.01 M PBS (pH 7.0), respectively. Finally, the SWV technique was applied to record the electrochemical responses for individual and simultaneous measurements of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE in HAc/NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption study of reactive dye on AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of reactive dye (Orange G and Carminic acid) on the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs was studied by using batch adsorption protocol. All adsorption tests were carried out in 0.1 M phosphate buffer at pH 5. A general procedure was as follows, 1.0 mg mL−1 of adsorbent (AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs) was mixed with 1.0 mL of 2.0 × 10-3 M OG (or CA). Then, the sample volume was adjusted to 5 mL with phosphate buffer (pH 5). Subsequently, the mixtures were shaken for 5 h at room temperature to reach equilibrium. In this case, the adsorption tests were in triplicates. The obtained hybrid was first thoroughly rinsed with distilled water to remove non-attached OG (or CA). Afterwards, the prepared products were thoroughly dispersed in 2 mL of distilled water. The as-synthesized reactive dye-loaded composites were denoted as AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG and AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@CA. The optimal conditions for the adsorbent of reactive dye in this study are pH 5, contact time = 5 h, adsorbent dose = 1 mg mL−1, temperature = 25 ± 1 °C and dye concentration (OG or CA) = 4.0 × 10-4 M.

For characterization of the nanomaterials four experimental methods were employed: UV–vis analysis, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis, Raman analysis, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis.

The UV–vis spectra of the as-synthesized EDA-CDs exhibit a absorption peak at around 360 nm and 240 nm ascribable to the p–p* transition. (Figure not shown). The phase architecture of the CDs was introduced by XRD (Fig. S1A, S = supplement). The characteristic XRD pattern exhibits a large-scale diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 26°, which is near to the graphite (002) crystal plane at 26°. On the other hand, the appeared sharp diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 49° is the closest to the graphite (102) plane at 50°. These consequences display that the bulk crystallinity of the CDs is uncompleted and the diminishes in the diffraction angles implicate improves in the CD (002) and (102) plane distance, which are created by the CO and CN bonds in the CDs.

To sense the graphitization grade of the CDs and the types of functional groups they include, Raman spectroscopy and FTIR analyses were performed. It has been declared that the defect-rich CNTs simplify the production of catalytic active sites for improved electrocatalytic activity. The defect-rich attribute of the CNTs is distinct from its high D-peak/G-peak ratio in RS. As illustrated in Fig. S1B, they both exhibit two typical peaks of CNTs. From the result, it is obviously observed in Fig. S1B that all of the samples comprises two characteristic RS peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1. The RS peak at around 1350 cm−1 is the D band (defect peak) and is attributed to the present defects, like disruption in the sp2 bonding. The RS peak at around 1560 cm−1 is the G band (graphite peak). The G band is distinctive of all graphitic architectures, originating owing to the inplane bond-stretching action of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [52]. The peak ratio of D-band to G-band (ID/IG) is proportional to disorder carbon materials and grows in value with breeding defect sites. The ID/IG ratio of the MWCNTs is the smallest (0.47), which affirms a most ordered graphitic structure. The CD/MWCNTs exhibits the largest ID/IG ratio (0.78). In this case, the ID/IG ratio increased from 0.47 to 0.78. The results demonstrate that the presence of CDs on the MWCNTs produced more structural defects on the MWCNTs, which mostly attributed to sp2 carbons transformed to sp3 hybridization[53]. In this instance, the escalated defect ratio can result in greater electrocatalytic activity.

FTIR analyses were carried out to determine the functional groups on EDA, EDA-CDs, and EDA-CDs-MWCNTs. Fig. S1C shows the FTIR spectra of pure EDA (spectrum a), EDA-CDs (spectrum b) and EDA-CDs-MWCNTs (spectra c). As shown in Fig. S1C (spectrum b), the EDA-CDs (spectrum b) exhibit many significant peaks of EDA. The FTIR profile of the EDA-CDs showed a broad absorption peak at 3230 cm−1 that is combined with the NH bond stretching, which overlapped with the OH stretching present in CDs. The NH stretching peak is observed at 1551 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum that is ascribed to the NH in-plane deterioration for primary amines. The modification of CDs with EDA is approved with the manifestation of a new peak at 1636 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching of the amide carbonyl group (N-CO) and the absence of the band at 1710 cm−1 assigned to CO in the –COOH groups. Furthermore, a sharp peak united with amide linkage (–CONH-) establish at 1636 cm−1 informs that plentiful EDA should be decorated at the CD surfaces by the amide linkages. On the other hand, when ox-MWCNTs was modified by EDA-CDs, several new peaks were exhibited in the spectrum. In the EDA-CD-MWCNTs spectrum (Fig. S1C, spectrum c), distinct peaks appearing at 1644, 1556, 1411, 1370 and 1306 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CN stretching vibrations combined with the out-of-plane NH2 and NH modes. The FTIR profiles verify that EDA-CDs were attached to the MWCNTs.

TEM images of EDA-CDs (A), ox-MWCNTs (B), and EDA-CDs-MWCNTs (C) are illustrated in Fig. 3 . It can be displayed that the EDA-CDs (Fig. 3A) are globular in morphology, and they are independent from each other, informing that the EDA-CDs are well dispersed in water. Fig. 3B displays the as-oxidized MWCNTs where the tubular architecture of MWCNTs is observed. By combining CDs with ox-MWCNTs, a great number of CDs were uniformly wrapped on ox-MWCNTs with negligible nanoparticle gathering, forming the EDA-CDs-MWCNTs hybrids, as presented in Fig. 3C. No free EDA-CDs were observed outside the MWCNTs, which also affirmed that the EDA-CD-MWCNTs were prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of assembling process of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the stepwise fabrication of sandwich-type immunosensor, Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was practised. Fig. 4 A shows the EIS performance of the modified electrode at each loading step. The EIS profile comprises two regions: (1) a semi-circle part at high frequencies and (2) a linear part at low frequencies. The first part defines the electron transfer process (semi-circle diameter = Ret) and the latter part shows the diffusion process. For the bare SPCE, the Ret value was found to be 2426 Ω (curve a). After the SPCE was coated with AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs on the surface, a lower resistance (the smaller diameter) was exhibited, which is ascribed to the phenomenon that the good conductibility of the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs has a lower Ret than unmodified SPCE. In brief, the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs can stimulate the electron transfer feature, and ultimately, the Ret value is reduced from 2426 Ω to 97 Ω. However, when anti-CYFRA 21-1 (Ab1)/anti-NSE (Ab1) (binary mixture) were attached to the surface of the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE, the Ret value increased to 697 (curve c) due to the non-conductive nature of the Ab1 molecules at the electrode surface. In other words, the semicircle increased remarkably (curve c) because Ab1 was immobilized on the electrode successfully and blocked the electron transfer. Following that, the modified electrode platform was incubated in BSA (1 %) solution, the Ret value increased again up to 1025 Ω (curve d). A larger semicircle diameter in curve d is seen, confirming the high resistance of the electrode interface as the developed immunosensor is blocked with BSA. Furthermore, when the immunosensor platform was incubated with antigen (CYFRA 21-1 and NSE) molecules (binary mixture), the Ret increased to about 1583 Ω (curve e) indicating the successful capture of antigen and the formation of an immunocomplex layer blocking electron transfer. However, when the redox probe (anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab2)- AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG and anti-NSE(Ab2)-AuNPs- EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@CA) solution was immobilized, the Ret value decreased to 343 Ω (curve f). This reason was that the electron mediator and AuNPs were excellent electrically conducting materials, which made electron transfer easier. The above EIS tests illustrate that the sandwich-type immunoassay system was successfully constructed.

The stepwise fabrication of the sandwich-type immunoassay was further confirmed by CV in 3 mM [Fe(CN)6]3- /4- containing 0.1 M KCl and the CV profiles are displayed in Fig. 4B. Curve a shows the response of the bare SPCE, from which a pair of redox current signals was emerged due to the oxidation/reduction reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. After the modification of AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs, the peak current of the modified SPCE (curve b) was remarkably higher than that of the SPCE (curve a). We find that the current of the capture antibody (Ab1: anti-CYFRA 21-1 and NSE modified electrode (anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1) and anti-NSE(Ab1)/ AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs (curve c) was lower than that of the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE (b), which was attributed to the poor electron transfer ability of Ab1. Similar to anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1) and anti-NSE(Ab1)/AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE, the current signal decreased with the stepwise addition of the BSA molecules (curve d). After the electrode was incubated in a binary mixture of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE molecules (curve e), the peak current decreased and the reason is that the biomolecules can establish poor conductive layers. Furthermore, incubated with the mixture of redox probe anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab2)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG and anti-NSE(Ab2)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@CA) solution (binary mixture), the current response increased (curve f). With this sandwich-type assay format, the Ab1-antigen–Ab2 immunocomplex was formed on the surface of the AuNPs- EDA-CDs-MWCNTs /SPCE. The CV findings indicated that the sandwich-type immunosensor had formed successfully and could be used in the proposed immunoassay. These results were in accordance with the EIS results.

To obtain good efficiency of immunosensor, some key factors were inspected (such as reactive dye adsorption, pH value, incubation temperature and time, BSA concentration). All these conditions were attentively optimized. The SWV measurement was taken from −0.3 V to 0.5 V with a pulse size of 150 mV, frequency of 5 Hz, and step size of 4 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and evaluation of redox-active species adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: Orange G and carminic acid were loaded at the surface of the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs to serve as a redox probe and increase the ECI signal due to the perfect conductivity and the large surface area of the EDA-CDs-MWCNTs. The redox-active species (dye or metal ion) commonly need to be attached to nanomaterials such as carbon, polymer, noble metal, and magnetic-based nanoparticles for further antibody immobilization and response enhancement [3,32–35]. There are many factors affecting dye adsorption such as concentration, solution pH value, and contact time. Thus, the effects of these crucial parameters are to be taken into account. Batch experiments were carried out to examine the adsorption of reactive dye. In this study, the pH of the reactive dye solution (OG and CA) is a key parameter that influences the adsorption process. Equilibrium experiments for the adsorption process onto the AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs were carried out at 25 ± 1 °C by taking 1 mL of 2.0 × 10-3 M reactive dye (OG or CA, each 2.0 × 10-3 M) with an adsorbent amount of 1 mg and at the different pH levels ranging from 3 to 7. In this case, the pH can be adjusted up to the desired value using a 0.1 M phosphate buffer and the volume was diluted to 5 mL distilled water. The resulting product AuNPs- EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG or (CA) were carefully separated using a centrifugation process and washed well with distilled water and then dispersed in 2 mL of distilled water. As the results shown in Fig. S2 indicate pH 5 is suitable for both dyes and was selected as optimum. Furthermore, the effects of dye concentration on the response of sensors were examined in the range of 1 × 10-5–8 × 10-4 M. It was observed that the immunosensor response was almost constant in the concentration range of 4 × 10-4-8 × 10-4 M for OG and 4 × 10-4-8 × 10-4 M for CA. In this way, a dye concentration of 4 × 10-4 M was selected and used in all further immunosensor experiments. To achieve efficient adsorption, the contact time was optimized. The contact time (sorbent-dye interaction) was tested at a time from 2 to 6 h. The results indicated that when contact time is raised from 5 to 6 h, the response scales are almost constant. The results observed that the required contact time for the adsorption process was found to be 5 h for the present adsorption system. The pH of 5.0, dye concentration of 4 × 10-4 M, contact time of 5 h, and temperature of 25 ± 1.0 °C were the optimum parameters for the immunoprobe activities. Accordingly, in this immunosensor system, pH 5.0, with an incubation time of 5 h, the adsorbent mass of 1 mg was chosen for use in the next experiments.

The pH was one of the key factors for the sandwich-type immunosensor, which was studied by SWV in a 0.1 ng mL−1 antigens mixed solution (binary solution). Fig. S3 shows the effect of pH values on the response. Fig. S3A displays the current responses of two antigens recorded on anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1), anti-NSE(Ab1)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE. It was found that the peak potentials of redox mediators (OG and CA) all shifted negatively with the increasing pH value from 3.6 to 6.0 at a 1.0 pH interval. The results indicated that proton was involved in the redox reaction of OG and CA (namely CYFRA 21-1 and NSE). As displayed in Fig. S3A, the current response of OG and CA was increased with the pH level increased from 3.6 to 5.0. However, the current responses reduced gently with the growth of pH from 5.0 to 6.0. When the pH is 5.0, the peak current of OG and CA reached the maximum value and two well-defined and sensitive peaks were observed when 0.1 M HAc/NaAc solution (pH 5.0) was used as an electrolyte. Fig. S3B shows pH dependencies of the peak currents of two markers. Therefore, the proper pH value for the individual and simultaneous sensing of two antigens was selected as 5.0.

Additionally, OG shows an SWV peak at + 0.012 V in pH 5 of acetate buffer solution and other than this didn't have any SWV response within the OG (or CYFRA 21-1) potential window. While, CA has three SWV peaks at Ep1 = 0.224 V, Ep2 = 0.344 V, and Ep3 = 0.827 V. As can be seen in Fig. S3C, one peak at Ep1 = 0.224 V, arising from the hydroquinone (5,8-di-hydroxyl) moiety, second peak at Ep2 = 0.344 V, arising from the catechol (5,6- di-hydroxyl) moiety, and third peak at Ep3 = 0.827 V arising from the 3-OH group. CA peak3 relates to oxidation on the 3-OH group of the CA-ring. Fig. S3C depicts the SWV intensity of the immunosensor towards the sensing of NSE. On the other hand, peak1 (Ep1 = 0.224 V) shows the higher peak current than peak2 (Ep2 = 0.344 mV) and peak3 (Ep3 = 0.827 V). Therefore, in this approach, the peak1 value was selected for the quantitative evaluation of NSE. Our findings support the work of de Souza Gil et al. [54]. For 3.6 < pH less than 6.0 the peak1 and 2 potentials were shifted to more negative values with increasing pH (Fig. S3C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation time on immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: The incubation time is a crucial factor in forming immunocomplex on the surface of the immunosensor platform. Therefore, the influence of the incubation length on the antigen–antibody interaction on the electrode surface was examined. Fig. S4 shows the peak currents after incubating CYFRA 21-1 and NSE on the anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1), anti-NSE (Ab1)- AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE electrodes for various periods (10–60 min). It could be observed that the current density of binary mixture (CYFRA 21-1/NSE) escalated with increasing incubation time and arrived at a stable value (a plateau) when the incubation time was longer than 50 min, expressing a saturated linking between the Ab1-antigen and Ab2 (namely Ab1-antigen-Ab2 immunocomplex) on the immunoelectrode surface. In other words, the response is saturated at 50 min, resulting in the optimal antigen–antibody linking. Our findings support the work of Zhou et al. [55]. On the other hand, extended incubation can cause antibody-antigen complexes to decompose [56]. Therefore, subsequent individual and simultaneous electrochemical experiments exploited 50 min as the most favourable incubation time and were used for the remaining experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation temperature on immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: The current response of the antigen–antibody interaction depends greatly on the temperature, thus, the influence of incubation temperature on the electrochemical response of the immunoassay was examined at different temperatures ranging from 25 to 60 °C in the presence of 0.1 ng mL−1 CYFRA 21-1/0.1 ng mL−1 NSE using SWV. The test results indicated that the response escalated with increasing incubation temperature up to 40 °C. However, further temperature increases led to a decrease in the current response. This case can be explained as follows: During the heating temperature increases from 25 °C to 40 °C, numerous antibody-antigen reactions are occurred and reduced the peak intensity, whereas higher heating above 40 °C may cause irreversible denaturation of CYFRA 21-1/NSE and anti-CYFRA 21-1/anti-NSE involved in the process [34,57]. Most antibodies interact best with the corresponding antigens at 37 °C and the velocity of their interaction (antibody-antigen) is also increased at 37 °C [56]. Hence, in this system, the most favorable incubation temperature of 37 °C was chosen as the proper incubation temperature.

Blocking treatment is one of the most crucial steps when performing immunosensor experiments. BSA agent is one of the most widely used blocking agents. In this study, BSA was employed to block free active sites and prevent nonspecific linking. Therefore, the effect of BSA concentration on the CYFRA 21-1 and NSE sensing sensitivity was also studied. In this case, the blocking agent (BSA) concentrations tested ranged from 0.1 % to 2 % BSA (w/v). In the concentration range of 0.1 % to 1.0 % BSA, the current response of analytes remained practically constant. Furthermore, the peak value of analytes decreased gradually with increasing BSA concentration. These findings indicated that the suitable concentration of BSA was 1 % w/v. On the other hand, excess amount of BSA (1 % w/v) is composed of too many double layer charges that accomplish a small current signal change of formation of antigen–antibody immunocomplexes. In other words, with the increase in BSA concentration, too many blocking molecules (BSA) were easily sorbed on the nanomaterial surface, leading to strong steric repulsion which finally predominated and stabilized the nanomaterial [58].

An impeccable immunosensor for individual or simultaneous electrochemical sensing of cancer markers must avoid cross-reactivity. Therefore, sequential conversely binary mixtures have been prepared with one antigen at a fixed concentration and the other antigen at varying concentrations in HAc/NaAc (0.1 M, pH 5). The tests were carried out on an anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1), anti-NSE(Ab1)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE, and the SWV profiles are illustrated in Fig. S5 (A and B). By fixing the NSE concentration at 0.1 ng mL−1 and varying the CYFRA 21-1 concentration in the range 0.0–5.0 ng mL−1 (namely 0, 0.1, 0.5, 2.0, and 5.0 ng mL−1), a log-linear increase (log concentration-effect) in the SWV peak current for the CYFRA 21-1 while steady ones have been exhibited for the immutable concentration of NSE in all compositions, as shown in Fig. S5 A. Moreover, by fixing the CYFRA 21-1 concentration at 0.1 ng mL−1 and varying the NSE concentration in the range 0.0–5.0 ng mL−1 (namely 0, 0.1, 0.5, 2.0, and 5.0 ng mL−1), a log-linear increase in the SWV response for the NSE. The obtained regression equations were: I (µA) = 2.7081 log CCYFRA 21-1 (ng mL−1) + 7.6613 (R2 = 0.9922) and I (µA) = 2.593 log CNSE (ng mL−1) + 4.2651 (R2 = 0.9938). All the experimental findings demonstrated that changing the concentration of an analyte has no obvious influence on the sensing of the other antigen and eventually, the cross-reactivity could be ignored. From both Fig. S5A and S5B, it can be demonstrated that the increase in the peak current was due to the increase in the concentration of any one of the antigens, and the other antigen exhibited constant peak current which defined that interference-free sensing is possible and the invented sandwich-type sensing method was good for selective simultaneous quantification of both antigens without into the reference of each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual and simultaneous detection of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical sensing of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE (single analyte) was first examined at the anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1), anti-NSE(Ab1)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE using SWV in pH 5 HAc/NaAc individually, as shown in Fig. 5 (A and B). In all, the corresponding calibration plot showed a good linear relationship between SWV signal and logarithm of the analyte concentration (the relation between Ip versus log C). Fig. 5 (A and B) shows SWVs of different concentrations of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE (single-analyte). The inset of Fig. 5 (A and B) shows the plot of the peak current (I p) as a function of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE concentrations. In the case of the electrochemical sensing of CYFRA 21-1, a linear response over the range from 0.002 to 10 ng mL−1 was found with the following linear regression: I(µA) = 2.755 log CCYFRA 21-1 (ng mL−1) + 7.9074 (R2 = 0.9906). The calculated LOD was found to correspond to 0.22 pg mL−1. For the sensing of NSE, a linear response was observed over the range from 0.002 to 10 ng mL−1 and the regression equation was I (µA) = 1.7497 log CNSE (ng mL−1) + 4.7805 (R2 = 0.9912). The LOD was found to correspond to 0.15 pg mL−1.

Simultaneous electrochemical quantification of both markers (binary solutions) was realized by simultaneously varying the concentrations of the targets and recording the SWVs using the anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1)/anti-NSE(Ab1)-AuNPs- EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE. From the inspection of Fig. 5C, it is obvious that the SWV intensity increases with improving concentrations of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE with the suitable calibration plots depicted as insets in Fig. 5C. The results indicate well-defined oxidation peaks at potentials of 24 mV for CYFRA 21-1, 236, and 332 mV for NSE, corresponding to the oxidation intensity of OG and CA, respectively. The results pointed out that the simultaneous electrochemical sensing of two markers was possible. The inset of Fig. 5 C displays the plot of the peak current (I p) as a function of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE concentrations. The calibration plots (Ip versus log C) (inset) were linearly related to CYFRA 21-1 and NSE over the ranges from 0.002 to 10 ng mL−1 and the regression equations were: I (µA) = 2.8225 log CCYFRA 21-1 (ng mL−1) + 8.052 (R2 = 0.9920) and I (µA) = 1.7846 log CNSE (ng mL−1) + 4.7275 (R2 = 0.9902) for CYFRA 21-1 and NSE, respectively. The LODs were 0.25 pg mL−1 for CYFRA 21-1 and 0.20 pg mL−1 for NSE, respectively. According to the findings, the invented sandwich-type ECI displayed a relatively wide linear response range and low sensing limit.

For comparison, the major characteristics (including linear sensing range and sensing limit) of EIs for individual and simultaneous sensing of both analytes (CYFRA 21-1 and NSE) were listed in Table S1, The method LOD values of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE were found to be between 0.15 pg mL−1 and 0.25 pg mL−1, which are relatively lower than available recent literature reports as listed in Table S1. It has been observed that anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1)-anti-NSE(Ab1)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCE has demonstrated a good analytical performance compared with other reported electrodes.

Selectivity is a crucial property of sandwich-type immunosensor and for this reason, it is required to test it for the invented electrochemical immunoassay. To test the selectivity of the immunosensor, we selected possible foreign substances, including AFP, CEA, PSA, CA 19–9, AA, UA, and DA for this investigation. The signal intensity was recorded in the presence of various foreign substances. The interfering effect of very high concentrations of various substances existing in real serum samples on the simultaneous assessment of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE has been investigated. For this aim, five different solutions were prepared in which the concentration of two markers (CYFRA21-1 and NSE each with a concentration of 0.1 ng mL−1) was kept constant, and concentrations of interferent were 1.0, 10, 25, 50, and 100 folds of the analyte concentration, using the mass ratios. Next, the mixture containing anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab2)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@OG and anti-NSE(Ab2)-AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs@CA probes was incubated with the electrodes, and then the current response was measured. 1.0, 10, 25, 50, and 100 folds of the substances did not affect CYFRA21-1 and NSE signal significantly. The current response variation caused by the interferential noncognate substances was less than 5 %, demonstrating that the invented immunosensor possesses a good selectivity for CYFRA21-1 and NSE. These findings have confirmed the invented electrode anti-interference feature toward the individual and simultaneous evaluation of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE.

To evaluate the reproducibility of the modified electrode, five anti-CYFRA 21-1(Ab1), anti-NSE(Ab1)- AuNPs- EDA-CDs-MWCNTs/SPCEs were prepared via the same fabrication method and used for the simultaneous sensing of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) for CYFRA 21-1 and NSE were 4.82 % and 5.07 %, respectively. The immunosensor stored at 4 °C for seven days showed that the steady-state peak current for the detection of 0.1 ng mL−1 CYFRA 21-1 and NSE, was 94.6 % and 96.5 % of the initial steady-state current response, respectively. Furthermore, after 15 and 25 days, the current response to the same CYFRA 21-1 and NSE concentration of the immunosensor decreased to about 84 % and 90 % of its initial value, respectively. Thus, the stability of the invented immunosensor was also acceptable. The findings indicated that the sandwich-type immunosensor had good electrochemical activity, acceptable sensor stability, and good reproducibility.

To further check the potential application of the invented immunosensor for practical analysis, both the amount of CYFRA21-1 and NSE in the samples and the recovery of the corresponding standard solution were measured. The two target biomarkers were not detected in the standard serum sample. The EC assay results were illustrated in Table 1 , each result was the average of three measurements. None of the biomarkers was identified in the standard serum sample. The recovery values were 98 %, 102 %, 98 % for CYFRA21-1 and 102 %, 102 %, 98 % for NSE, respectively. the RSDs were 2.0 %, 3.9 %, 1.4 % for CYFRA21-1 and 1.4 %, 2.8 %, 1.2 % for NSE, respectively. The recovery values of CYFRA21-1 and NSE at three different concentrations were 98 %-102 % and 102 %-105 %, respectively, such good recovery values with low relative standard deviations indicate that this immunosensor could be further employed for clinical detection of CYFRA21-1 and NSE. To more deeply check how feasible and reliable the invented immunosensor is in clinical serum samples, it is used to determine five serum samples of patients with lung cancer. The levels of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE were analyzed by assaying clinical samples containing CYFRA 21-1 and NSE and following that, the data from this immunoassay approach were reliably compared with those from the ELISA test. The assay results are manifested in Table 2 . The RSDs between the two methods were less than 5.14 % for CYFRA 21-1 and less than 6.85 % for NSE, indicating an acceptable accuracy and this developed immunoassay approach could be applied in the electrochemical detection of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE. In addition, for the clinical serum sample, the data from this immunosensor are consistent with those from enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). As a result, the invented immunosensor can be employed for the individual and simultaneous quantification of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE. Thus, it shows that the immunosensor is expected to be used in clinical diagnosis.

As can be seen in Table 2, serum levels of CYFRA 21-1 and NSE higher than 3.3 and 5 ng/mL (healthy marker levels), respectively, were considered elevated. The fact that CYFRA 21-1 is a specific marker for squamous cell carcinoma, suggests that elevated levels of serum CYFRA 21-1 indicate the presence of squamous cell components. Furthermore, CYFRA 21-1 level indicate higher stage cancer in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) [59]. Also, elevated serum levels of NSE can be assumed to be specific to neuronal tumors (particularly neuroblastomas, small cell carcinomas of the lung, malignant gliomas, and so on) [60]. On the other hand, a high level of a tumor marker may be a sign of cancer or certain benign (noncancerous) conditions.

According to the above results, a sensitive sandwich-type electroanalytical procedure for the individual and simultaneous evaluation of CYFRA21-1 and NSE has been successfully created. Assembling the advantageous attributes of AuNPs and EDA-CDs-MWCNTs, a hybrid architecture has been established on the surface of SPCE forming an unusual composite film. The AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs were served as the sensing immunoplatform for the fixing of primary antibodies (anti-CYFRA21-1 and anti-NSE). Furthermore, hybrid AuNPs-EDA-CDs-MWCNTs with copiously active groups and the porous texture can engage a large number of redox-active species (namely dyes), therefore, amplifying the peak signals of immunosensor. Dye labels provide many benefits, as they are highly sensitive responses and stable over a long time, and do not interfere with the function of the target markers. The invented ECI has demonstrated resplendent electroanalytical responses toward cancer markers at very small levels. Moreover, this invented ECI was enforceable to detect CYFRA21-1 and NSE levels in clinical samples provided by clinical patients, which had good accordance with the ELISA. This sandwich-type ECI is unprecedented and can be used as a reliable sensing protocol for exhaustive observation and precise diagnosis of lung cancers.

Handan Kamış: Writing – review & editing.
